system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.

Pattern 29 —- All Chord Types (Chromatic Approach Notes)

The technique of pattern 29 is often used in gypsy jazz.
Each note of the F triad used in this pattern is approached from a chromatic note below.

Maj7 chords (or major triads) are a common substitution for minor chords.

You can play a major triad or maj7 chord on the third of a major chord.
For example: Fmaj7 (or F) over Dm7

coronene I

Played over Dm7

© Play audio example

Pattern 35 — G7 or Dm7 (Augmented)

Pattern 35 features either an augmented triad or a maj7#5 arpeggio (check out pattern
4 and pattern 70 for more use of the augmented sound).

Augmented Over Minor Chords

Augmented chords are often used to play over minor chords, resulting in a minor/major 7 sound.

Over a minor chord you can play an augmented triad or maj7#5 arpeggio, both built on the b3

of the minor chord.
For example: Gaug or Gmaj7#5 over Em7

G augmented triad

Gmaj7#5 arpeggio

Played over Em7

Here’s how a G augmented triad looks like on the fretboard:

Gaug



The convenience about augmented triads is that its inversions look exactly the same, so you can just
slide the same shape 4 frets up or down and essentially get the same chord:

Here’s how a Gmaj7#5 arpeggio looks like:

Gmaj7#5



Btw, if you strum this chord (Gmaj7#5/E = Em/maj9) from low to high, you will be reminded of a
certain movie:

Em/maj9

7 9

Augmented Over Dominant #11 Chords

Augmented triads can also be used to solo over dominant #11 chords, which are usually the result
of a tritone substitution. For example: Db7#11 going to Cmaj7

Because Db7#11 is a substitution for G7alt, you can use a G augmented triad over that chord.

Maj7#5 arpeggios can also be used over half-diminished chords and gives you a natural 9 sound
Over a half-diminished chord, you can play a maj7#5 arpeggio built on the b5 of the m7b5

Pattern 36 — Major (and Dominant) 1235

The 1235 pattern (aka the Coltrane pattern) is a four-note motif that consists of the 1st, 2nd, 3rd,
and 5th notes of a scale.

These patterns were popularized by John Coltrane, who used them on his Giant Steps solo.
Let’s start with the major version of the 1235 pattern.

Because the 1235 pattern doesn’t have a 7 or b7, it can be used over major as well as dominant
chords.
